data.title: United States Lime & Minerals: Great Company, But No Margin Of Safety
data.description:
Investment Thesis

United States Lime & Minerals, Inc. (USLM) is a lime- and limestone-producing company. For the last 5 years, it has shown improvements in revenue growth. Revenues rose at a CAGR of 14.6% from 2020 to 2024. Cost of revenue margins decreased from 78% in 2015 to 55% in 2024. Operating margins increased from 21% in 2020 to 39% in 2024. The business has no debt, and most of its assets are allocated in current assets, of which 79% is cash.

Despite the cyclicality of the industries USLM serves, the company’s financial history shows resilience during market downturns. During the Great Financial Crisis, from 2008 to 2009, revenues dropped 12.5% and then recovered 13.4% from 2009 to 2010. Operating margins improved during that time (16.4%, 17.9%, and 20.9% in 2008, 2009, and 2010). In the COVID-19 pandemic, revenues increased by 1.5% from 2019 to 2020, and operating margins went from 18.5% to 21.1%; margins boomed in 2021 to 37%.

Thanks to the increase in revenues, operating efficiencies, a great balance sheet, and solid fundamentals, the stock soared ~1,160.5%. It went from a low of $12.60 (split-adjusted) in March 2020 to an all-time high price of $160 (split-adjusted) in November 2024. The stock then corrected 48.65% at the beginning of the year, then recovered 61.98%. It went from a low of $82.98 in March to $134.43 in October 2025.

However, the current valuations of this business don’t provide a margin of safety at the current price of $119 per share (as of December 3rd, 2025), and caution for long-term investors is recommended. For example, Microsoft in the 2000s was a great business, but it was very overvalued. As value investors, we don’t want to overpay for assets, even if they are great. Instead, we buy great companies at low or fair prices.

An in-depth analysis reveals the market is pricing in very high growth, which might not materialize when considering USLM’s core market, its positioning in the industry, and the company’s history. At a current P/E multiple of ~27x and a stock price of $119 (as of December 3rd, 2025), the company is overvalued.

Company Overview

USLM is a lime and limestone producer and seller that was founded in Dallas, Texas, in 1945. The company went public in 1995 and was listed on the NASDAQ Composite Index. It produces and sells pulverized limestone, or PLS, aggregate, hydrated lime, and lime slurry from its high-quality limestone open-pit quarries and underground mines.

USLM mines and produces crushed limestone, which is sold as aggregate material for construction. PLS is used in construction to produce roof shingles, asphalt paving, and the production of glass. It’s also used as an additive to agricultural feeds and soil enhancement. It’s used for flue gas treatment for desulfurization systems in coal-fired power plants, as well as other industries that require scrubbing of emissions for environmental and mine safety purposes (2024 10-K).

Quicklime is used in the metal industry to process steel. It’s used for soil stabilization in highways, roads, building construction, oilfield roads, and drill sites. It’s used in municipal sanitation, water treatment facilities, and to manufacture paper products. Hydrated lime is used in construction, flue gas and water treatment, in asphalt as an anti-stripping agent, in the production of chemicals, and as a conditioning agent for oil and gas drilling mud. Lime slurry is mainly used in construction.

Mine/Locations

  • Texas Lime Quarry (Texas)
  • Batesville Quarry (Arkansas)
  • Love Hollow Quarry (Arkansas)
  • St. Clair Mine (Oklahoma)
  • Carthage Mine (Missouri)
  • Mill Creek Quarry (Oklahoma)
  • Monarch Pass Quarry (Colorado)

USLM can reach customers within a radius of ~400 miles of each of the company’s plants.

USLM has a great position in the south-centric regions of the country. This has allowed it to maintain its customer base, endure, and thrive in its highly competitive, concentrated, and high-barrier-to-entry market. This is thanks to the scarcity of high-quality limestone deposits, for which zoning and permitting are required for the extraction of the stone materials that can be mined, produced, and sold (F2023 10-K).

The company highlights the need for lime plants and facilities located close to markets and relevant industries to reinforce the premium value of operations. This includes having permitted, long-term, high-quality limestone reserves with great locations and transportation, where demand is high and sustainable.

ULSM possesses a narrow moat, thanks to its strategic locations, low-cost production, and the high barriers to entry. However, we’ll see shortly that despite being a great company, the current valuation doesn’t provide a margin of safety for long-term investors.

Valuation Analysis

Following Bruce Greenwald's valuation methodology, I will present a Net Asset Value model (NAV) and an Earnings Power Value model (EPV).

Net Asset Value

To determine the NAV, I calculated the reproduction cost of its assets. The most difficult assets to value were USLM’s mineral reserves and land. Most of the information about the costs incurred to purchase them is not reported in the 10-Ks. I undertook the endeavor of estimating purchase prices based on past transactions disclosed by USLM and current listing prices for quarries in the US.

For mineral reserves and land, I calculated a 1.85x multiple to the 2024 book value. For proven natural gas properties, I applied a 1.5x multiple. For buildings, leasehold improvements, machinery and equipment, furniture and fixtures, and automotive equipment, I applied a ~0.85x multiple to account for depreciation and inflation.

All $ in millions

Intangibles are not accounted for in the balance sheet. But they must be accounted for.

Current liabilities remain the same as the latest current book value (Q3 2025), and the only adjustment is for deferred tax liabilities, which are valued at half of book value.

The NAV on an equity basis is $933.6 million. Adding back $0 of total debt and subtracting cash and non-core assets gives us an NAV on an enterprise basis of $583.7 million.

Earnings Power Value

USLM revenues increased rapidly from 2021 to 2025 because of the rapid increase in prices for lime and limestone products. The company has a stable customer base and volume of sales, which limits the revenue growth. Fortunately, this commodity is very stable, and prices increase rapidly during economic booms and remain stable during economic busts.

The correlation is evident. This allows us to estimate sustainable revenues.

A conservative estimate of sustainable revenues is $300 million. A sustainable operating margin is ~33%, and 19% should be a sustainable corporate tax rate. After adjusting to account for the cost of replacement capex, I computed a sustainable earnings power of $76.9 million. Let’s compute the cost of capital.

USLM has no debt. Thus, the cost of equity is the cost of capital. I believe USLM is at the low end of the normal-risk cyclical equities, but it also belongs to the high end of quality, low-leverage businesses. Therefore, 8% is an appropriate estimate of USLM’s cost of capital. The earnings power divided by the cost of capital yields an EPV of $961.2 million (EV basis) and $1,311.2 million on an equity basis.

Let’s compare NAV & EPV on both bases.

Case C company - NAV < EPV

This valuation yields an intrinsic value on an equity basis of $1,122.4 million, or $39 per share. But this is a Case C company (franchise businesses with an economic moat), therefore, the value of growth takes a greater weight on USLM’s valuation, rendering the NAV & EPV calculations pretty much useless, as they only point us to value the growth instead.

Franchise Value Model (For Case C Companies)

The models help us compute a benchmark return, which assumes that the current market cap and the intrinsic value are equal, and what the return an investor would get today if they bought equity from USLM.

The three components are cash return (net dividends and buybacks), the organic growth rate (growth rate of USLM’s core market), and the active reinvestment return. The sum of these rates, minus the fade rate, computes the benchmark return, which is then compared to the cost of capital to see if there is a margin of safety.

The current cash return for investors is 0.2%. The US lime and limestone market is expected to have a CAGR of 3.5% (in tandem with the average growth rate of the US GDP). The active reinvestment return is 4%, and the fade rate is about 0.9%. This gives us a benchmark growth return at the current market price of 6.8%. Compared to the cost of capital of 8%, we see that it’s lower. Therefore, there is no margin of safety.

Market Expectations Analysis

While we have thoroughly valued this company, I like to assess market expectations with a reverse DCF model.

Assuming the company maintains a 33% operating margin, COGS margins of 60.5%, a corporate tax rate of 19%, a cost of capital of 8%, and working capital trails revenue growth for the next 30 years, the market expects a ~5.5% revenue CAGR at a current price of $119 and $120.

Experts in the industry and historical trends indicate that the US lime and limestone market is expected to grow 3.5% in the long term, so how can USLM be expected to grow faster than the expected CAGR of its industry over the next 30 years? The scenario is unlikely. If that were to be the case, USLM would become the US lime and limestone market itself.

If we use the same model to yield a 3.5% CAGR, the estimated intrinsic value of equity is $2,721.2 million, or $95.3 per share. The current price would be overpriced by ~26%. This also means that if a 3.5% CAGR materializes, the market is overestimating revenue growth by ~57%.

Risks

Lime and limestone prices could keep rising rapidly for many years, since this is normal for this commodity. This could help USLM’s revenues to rise further than expected, and thanks to its strategic investments to improve operations, margins and earnings will be higher, helping the stock price to rise further. I could also be underestimating the future CAGR for the US lime and limestone industry, which could grow at a faster rate than previously expected. Maybe it does grow 5% per year, or even 6% per year, which would warrant a higher growth rate of return for USLM. Also, USLM could grow faster than its industry for some time, allowing the company to gain more market share (through more acquisitions and expansion). Another risk is that the benchmark growth return could be a lot higher; I could be underestimating either the organic growth rate or the active reinvestment return.

Recommendation

USLM is clearly a great company, and the recent increase in revenues piqued the interest of Wall Street and investors alike. Thanks to the improvement in revenues, operating efficiencies, operating margins, no debt, solid balance sheet, conservative management, modest competitive advantages (thanks to the geographical position of its limestone quarries and low-cost production), and its sporadic but successful investment strategy, the stock rose rapidly, providing fantastic returns for investors. However, an intelligent investor knows that no matter how great a company is, we shouldn’t pay great prices for it. Instead, for companies that consistently grow and provide value, we want to pay fair prices. How we determine those fair prices depends a lot on the valuation methods we use and the assumptions we make about growth.

I provide a different approach by calculating a benchmark return that can be compared to the cost of capital to determine a margin of safety. At the current market cap of $3.4 billion, this benchmark return is approximately 6.8%, which is less than the cost of capital of 8%. This offers no margin of safety for long-term investors, and a lower price is required to make USLM an attractive investment. All things equal, at a market cap of $1.5 billion, investors would get a 12.2% benchmark return for their investment in USLM, which would provide a margin of safety of 34.3%. For investors to get an equal benchmark return to the cost of capital, the market cap must be ~$2.65 billion.

On the other hand, if we take the riskier approach by trying to determine USLM’s revenue CAGR for the next 30 years and assume it will achieve a 3.5% growth rate per annum, the intrinsic value of equity would be $2.7 billion. Investors would need the market cap to drop to $1.8 billion to get a decent margin of safety.

For all these reasons, I give USLM an underweight (Sell) rating, with a fair value per share of $93–$95 per share.

data.title: Nikkei 225 Slips As SoftBank Slump And BOJ Hike Fear Pressure Tech Stocks
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

The Nikkei 225 fell 0.9% to close near 50,149 on Thursday as a sharp slide in SoftBank (SFTBY) erased early-session gains and dragged Japan’s heavyweight tech names lower. Oracle’s (ORCL) steep post-earnings decline spilled into Asian markets, triggering a 7% drop in SoftBank and weakening sentiment across semiconductor equipment makers, industrials and major consumer electronics stocks.

The session began on steady footing, but momentum cracked mid-day as SoftBank’s slide cascaded across the index. Lasertec (LSRCY), Fujikura (FKURF), Mitsubishi Heavy (MHVYF), Sanrio (SNROF) and Nintendo (NTDOY) all turned lower, reflecting a broad shift to risk-off positioning. Investors grew cautious not only because of the global tech earnings reset but also due to the possibility that the BOJ could introduce its first rate hike of the cycle. That combination left buyers reluctant to defend early advances.

Technical picture shows fatigue, but broader uptrend intact

From a structural standpoint, the Nikkei remains in an established uptrend, though recent price action shows signs of cooling. The index sits directly above the 20-day EMA at 50,061, a level that has served as a stabilizing pivot throughout the past month. The 50-day EMA at 48,871 holds further below, anchoring the medium-term trend.

However, repeated failures to push beyond the 51,500 region highlight fading momentum. RSI at 52 reflects neutrality rather than the high-energy thrust seen during the October breakout. Price ranges have tightened over the past two weeks, a classic sign that market participants are waiting for a clearer macro signal before committing to fresh positions.

Short-term structure is more fragile. On the 30-minute chart, the Supertrend flipped bearish after SoftBank’s slide triggered a sharp intraday decline. SAR dots remain above price, reinforcing downward pressure. An initial attempt to rebound from the 50,000 level stalled at resistance near the Supertrend line at 50,544. Until the index can reclaim that intraday pivot, short-term risks point toward retests of 49,900 and 49,700. These levels acted as key accumulation zones last week and will determine whether dip-buyers remain engaged.

Policy risks and global tech sentiment shape near-term direction

Despite the pullback, the broader backdrop for Japanese equities remains constructive. Corporate governance reforms, strong capital spending and steady foreign inflows have been reliable supports throughout the year. The long-term trend remains intact as long as the Nikkei holds above the 50-day EMA.

The immediate risk lies in policy divergence. A BOJ rate hike next week would widen the gap between Japanese tightening and global easing expectations. A firmer yen would pressure export-heavy and yen-sensitive sectors, which have powered the index’s run to historic highs. If the currency strengthens sharply, the Nikkei could find it harder to maintain its upper range.

Global tech sentiment also remains a swing factor. Oracle’s earnings-driven drop has shaken confidence in a sector that heavily influences the index. If U.S. megacaps remain volatile into next week’s data releases, Japanese tech could remain under pressure.

For now, the Nikkei stands at an inflection point. Holding above 50,000 would allow consolidation and another attempt at 51,500. Losing that shelf could unwind momentum toward the 48,800–49,000 region where the 50-day EMA and earlier breakout structure converge.

Outlook as traders watch BOJ decision

Market attention now shifts to whether today’s weakness was a SoftBank-specific shock or an early signal of broader hesitation ahead of next week’s BOJ meeting. The policy outcome, combined with global earnings sentiment and yen behavior, will determine whether the index resumes its upward trajectory or slips into a deeper consolidation phase.

Previously, we discussed how export strength, foreign inflows and improved governance kept Japan’s market resilient even during global tech volatility. The current pullback echoes that theme: the uptrend remains intact, but short-term direction hinges on whether tightening risks materialize.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Bank Of Canada Keeps Key Interest Rate Unchanged
data.description:
The Bank of Canada has kept its benchmark interest rate at 2.25%, in a move that was widely expected. Maria Solovieva, Economist at TD Bank Group, speaks with MoneyTalk’s Anthony Okolie about whether this latest decision marks the end of the central bank’s tightening cycle and what to expect from Canada’s economy in 2026.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: As widely expected, the Bank of Canada held its key interest rate steady at 2.25%, its last decision of 2025. Joining me now to discuss the latest decision is TD economist Maria Solovieva.

And Maria, what's your reaction to the decision? Any surprises for you?

Maria Solovieva: This was one of those decisions where you can sit back and relax, put your feet up, grab some popcorn, and enjoy the show. We knew that the most likely decision will be a hold, and Governor Macklem was very prescriptive in the previous statement that this is where he thinks, and the governing council thinks that where the economy is in a steady state, and it warrants the current rate of 2.25, which is at the lower range of the neutral rate.

Anthony Okolie: OK, so given this decision, does this mark the end of the Bank of Canada easing cycle, or is there a future rate cut a possibility?

Maria Solovieva: Well, actually, the market is pricing in a full hike by the end of next year by October 2026. And it has come down that pricing has come down a little bit. But we have to think the very strong jobs report last week to that, and indeed, it was a very nice surprise to see unemployment rate come down to 6.5%. And just remember that September was 7.1%.

Yeah, so this was quite unexpected. The economy added more jobs as well. So the market's got ahead of themselves and priced in a more hawkish stance. But today, we're seeing the reaction in the opposite direction with the two year yields coming down a little bit, that pricing adjusting a little bit as well.

So we would probably say that right now, where we are currently at that rate is where we should be. And I think the Bank of Canada is of the same opinion. And most likely we're going to hear this today at the press conference as well.

Anthony Okolie: So it's interesting that they're pricing in hikes. We still have a big uncertainty right now, which remains the tariff negotiations between Canada and the United States. What happens if we don't get a deal? What impact could that have on monetary policy next year?

Maria Solovieva: So we have it as a status quo right now. There is no deal. We haven't implemented anything going in the more of direction of a better deal than the current stance or worse deal than the current stance. So obviously, this is a risk. But I'm afraid that the Bank of Canada can't do much about it.

And again, they have been fairly clear about the fact that the tariff negotiations and tariff war in general is not something that they can lean against. So for them, this cycle is a little bit unusual. And we also agree with that, that you do have your typical slowing demand and your typical attributes of a typical cycle, but at the same time, you also have this headwinds that the Bank of Canada is just simply not equipped to deal with.

So unfortunately, even if there are some changes in the trade stance and to the worse, the Bank of Canada just simply won't be able to do much about it. So we think that they will remain just vigilant and watching some of the measures that are coming out of the economy.

Anthony Okolie: Now, one of the reasons why the Bank of Canada might be sitting on the sidelines is we've got the 2025 federal budget, which is now public. How do you see that playing out to help backstop the Canadian growth in 2026?

Maria Solovieva: We have implemented just a little bit of a boost. We do think that the budget is a nice first step in the right direction. There are definitely a lot of things that need to be done, and the government is trying to reorient the economy towards more trade with east-west versus south-north. And that's a very difficult task, right?

So there are some elements that we definitely welcome, some tax incentives, broadening out of tax incentives that are hopefully will make it a positive towards next year and mostly going forward as well. But we think that there is more to be done, and we're hoping that some deregulation, potential changes to taxes as well, little steps that will help make Canada more competitive.

There's obviously a little bit more work to be done also on the industrial level, like working with the companies, working with industries, understanding their needs as well, and trying to help them out. So there is way more work to be done, and we still need to see how this is going to play out in 2026.

Anthony Okolie: OK. So let's talk about currency. Given this latest decision, how do you see the loonie performing going forward?

Maria Solovieva: So it's funny because we actually did see a boost to the loonie just recently because of this expectation of a rate hike or just a little bit higher pricing of that. This was probably just too premature. But going forward in 2026, we do expect a bit of a stronger loonie just on the margin. And that comes from the interest rate differentials as we expect the Federal Reserve to cut rates and Bank of Canada staying on the same level. The price of the loonie will also improve. And that's what fundamentally we think that's the appropriate, 70, 73, 74 is where we're expecting it to be, US cents per Canadian dollar.

Anthony Okolie: So now going forward, what indicators do you think the Bank of Canada will be following closely as they prepare for the next rate decision in 2026?

Maria Solovieva: Well, inflation is clearly one of them. And the bank already stated in today's statement that they would be looking through most likely some of the upward pressures in inflation due to the HST tax last year. So the base effect will play out in the next couple of months.

So they will be cognizant of that and not necessarily react to that. So definitely inflation and our expectation as well is that it will gradually come down to the target level of 2%. Definitely inflation expectations of what consumers and businesses are expecting going forward. And I think so far, it's moving in the right direction. There's a little bit of upward pressure because of the tariff narrative, but I think overall it's fairly balanced.

And then, of course employment. This report, for example, that we saw last week for the month of November, it was actually showing more stronger economy, stronger job market. It will be interesting to see play out because some of this strength is actually coming from part time jobs. So it's not necessarily the fundamental strength that you would want to see.

So they would be looking at the job market and supply and demand. And from the supply perspective, we're actually expecting flat labor force next year. So that even if there is a bit of slack in the jobs market, you could actually still see favorable environment for the unemployment rate. So this would be interesting to see. But, the devil is always in the details. So they'll have to really dissect it to make sure that they understand the fundamental drivers of the economy.

Original Post

data.title: Oracle Fiscal Q2 Earnings: AI And Debt Concerns Remain
data.description:
Shares of Oracle Corporation (ORCL) have plunged 15% following the release of its fiscal second quarter results. This makes it so that shares are down 45% from the highs in September, but still trade higher than levels seen in June. This just goes to show the huge volatility and moves displayed by the shares over the past half a year.

Concerns are driven by a boom in RPOs; however, this is driven by low-creditworthy customers, huge capital investment requirements (and thus debt increases), as well as little additional revenue and uncertain margins to show for it.

This creates continued uncertainty, and while the recent correction has been fierce, shares still trade with healthy gains this year, making me very cautious here, as management has not been able to take away AI related concerns.

About The Base

Oracle is best known for its cloud and database services, and this business has seen modest but steady growth, having seen revenues increase by some 50% over the past decade to levels just shy of $60 billion.

Amidst low investment needs, stable margins, and huge cash flow generation, the company managed to reduce the share count by a third to 2.8 billion shares, delivering more solid growth on a per share basis. In fact, capital spending often trended at a couple of billion until recent years, that is until the AI boom arrived.

All of this drove a huge boom in the shares, which rose by a factor of 5 times from levels in the $40s in 2015 to $190 at this point. For a base, I looked at the fiscal 2025 results, that is, for the year ending in May of this year.

Revenues that year rose by 8% to $57.4 billion, growth largely driven by increasing cloud revenues. The company reported GAAP earnings of $12.4 billion, with adjusted earnings reported at $17.3 billion, with the difference relating to stock-based compensation and, to a smaller extent, amortization charges.

Net debt of $81 billion was substantial. This came after the company had taken on quite some debt for buybacks and dividends in the past years, but was also the result of capital spending for the year ballooning to more than $21 billion! At this time, shares broke the $200 mark, based on the anticipation of ballooning demand, creating more optimism than concerns.

This was in part evident by a 41% increase in remaining performance obligations, reported at $138 billion, as an indication of the accelerating growth to come. This growth was to a smaller extent visible in the fourth quarter results, with reported revenue up 11%, although aided by the impact of a softer dollar.

An Eventful Half A Year

In September, shares exploded as shares rose 50% in the time frame of just a few weeks to their highs. This was driven by a 359% increase in remaining performance obligations to $455 billion, this number up more than $300 billion on a sequential basis thanks to a partnership with OpenAI (OPENAI).

All that anticipated growth is not seen in the first quarter results for 2026, with reported sales up 12% to $14.9 billion, with growth coming in a point less in constant currency terms. Capital spending jumped to $8.5 billion, and as an illustration of how large this number is, this compares to just a $1.3 billion depreciation charge, with net capital investments of $7 billion and some being equal to half of revenues! Needless to say, this results in rapidly increasing net debt.

Real concern quickly arose thereafter around the contract with OpenAI, its terms, and the massive buildup in debt, including required capital spending in relation to GPUs. Other risks were the limited earnings power of the business (in the grand scheme of things) and the already net leveraged balance sheet ahead of the investment boom.

Second quarter remaining performance obligations rose further to $523 billion. As the business is quick to point out, this is based on other clients as well, including Meta Platforms (META) and Nvidia (NVDA), with the latter being somewhat of a question mark as a supplier but an off-taker as well.

Total second quarter revenues rose by 14% to $16.1 billion, and while this marks an acceleration, this still marks limited sales growth in relation to the investments made. Capital spending to date is reported at $20.5 billion, implying a $12 billion capex number for the second quarter! Net debt jumped to $88 billion, despite the business selling its stake in Ampere during the quarter, while the company is actually already diluting the shareholder base a bit, with the diluted share count up nearly 2% over the past year!

And Now?

With 2.9 billion shares trading around the $190 mark, Oracle has a $550 billion equity valuation, for a $640 billion enterprise value. This values the vast majority of the business, which is still a $60 billion database/cloud business at 10 times sales and around 40 times earnings, at around $15-$16 billion.

Needless to say, at this valuation, there are anticipated hopes of a growing business from AI, as shares have risen, but also the debt load has been rising, with investors being cautious about applying a huge premium to this new business.

In fact, with ORCL stock trading down some $150 from the highs, the market value of the firm has declined by $400 billion on the back of these concerns! This is driven by the right reasons. While cloud revenues rose by 34% in the second quarter and now make up half of sales, the actual costs of sales were up 45%, which resulted in some real deleveraging here.

The market reaction is a negative, and I am approaching the stock very cautiously as well. This comes in part as the capital spending forecast for the year has been hiked by fifteen billion to $50 billion, despite the comments that the company sees many financing options for its expansion. However, by now, the annual depreciation expense has risen to about $7 billion, still marking huge net capital investment requirements.

For now the company is actively trying to minimize the increase in net debt, including 2% dilution per annum and the sale of the stake in Ampere, but still this results in a huge $88 billion net debt load here.

It is this built up in debt, but also the huge net capital investments and uncertain margin profile on the investments, that make me very cautious here, as frankly many jitters are seen in the stock, which, however, is still up 30% year to date.

This remains a fascinating story to watch unfold, and despite the recent cautious trading action, I am very cautious, as frankly the ORCL investment remain overextended here.

data.title: 2026 S&P 500 Outlook: Inside The Next Earnings Wave
data.description:
Market Outlook

The S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) have pushed higher YTD and still don't resemble a late-cycle blow-off. My take is simple. The S&P 500 does not need valuation magic to move higher in 2026. It needs earnings follow-through and a macro backdrop that doesn’t interfere.

AI is now progressing from theory to profit, profits are increasing, and the macro environment is supportive, rather than bearish. A target of 8,000 in the optimistic side for the S&P 500 index has a robust foundation. Risks are still around, but cracks are not seen, rather, it looks like a rough patch. This cycle isn’t ending, it’s shifting from narrative to numbers.

Earnings: The Market’s New Center of Gravity

The most compelling reason for further gains, in my opinion, remains earnings. What used to be a story about what AI might do in the future is now showing up directly in company profits. The estimates for S&P 500 earnings in 2026 are seeing a low to mid-teens gain, which in itself is a step in the right direction from the fairly robust profitability that has been seen in recent years. An index of this magnitude seeing this type of acceleration rarely occurs by coincidence.

The key factor has been that AI has, at long last, started to transition from proof-of-concept into actual use. We are no longer discussing experiments in R&D budgets but fully integrated solutions transforming software, cloud infrastructure, logistics, healthcare, and finance. Executives who once spoke of “AI potential” now discuss speed, cost structures, and throughput. Production floors cut down-time with predictive analytics, stores turn inventories with better forecasting, and banks detect suspicious transactions that once required thousands of hours.

This is why many experts describe the start of an earnings-driven supercycle. It is not crystal-ball gazing but a realization that a new dimension of productivity has begun across industries. When revenue and margin lines improve together, markets respond robustly.

Therefore, earnings-driven markets are different from liquidity-driven markets, as in earnings-driven markets, markets are more resilient even in drawdowns and are also less dependent on market sentiment.

A Macro Environment that Assists Rather Than Impedes

Macro conditions do not need to be spectacular to support the market. They simply need to avoid creating headwinds strong enough to interrupt earnings progression. The conditions that exist as we enter 2026 satisfy this test. There are still moderate rates of growth, pressures on inflation are fading, and monetary policy tightness begins to ease into neutrality. Such a course of events has a potent impact that many overlook.

The US GDP is expected to grow slightly above 2% in the upcoming year, which, although not very exciting, represents the precise conditions under which earnings growth becomes effective. Nothing that excites the imagination, but also not hot enough to reignite inflation or trigger a recession. This represents a functional environment. Consumers are engaged. Balance sheets are in better shape than in previous periods of tighter monetary policy. Labor markets are no longer hot but are in a position to facilitate expenditures.

The Fed has already started making substantial cuts in interest rates in 2025, moving its policy stance to the mid-3% range. With inflation likely to move towards realizing its target of two percent, further cuts in 2026 are well on their cards. A small cut in interest rates also benefits longer-term sectors such as cloud, semiconductors, and AI infrastructure. A lower discount rate benefits such sectors as it induces higher valuations. However, the true benefit of a dovish Fed resides in psychology.

The Quiet Tailwind Powering the Cycle

One important driver that hasn’t received nearly enough attention is federal industrial and infrastructure policy. The push for technological competitiveness has created more than headlines as it has triggered multi-year spending across semiconductors, grid and energy infrastructure, clean energy, cybersecurity, and defense. These funding streams don’t arrive all at once, nor do they depend on short-term market cycles.

This matters because it helps smooth the business cycle. Even if private investment slows in certain sectors, federally backed projects keep demand steady. That’s especially true for manufacturers building data centers, companies expanding transmission networks, and cloud providers adding processing capacity. The result is a more predictable revenue environment that cushions these industries from sharp downturns. It won’t grab the same attention as GDP or inflation releases, but it functions as a quiet, steady, and meaningful tailwind.

The outcome is an economy that may not look explosive on the surface yet continues to support solid earnings growth underneath. That combination, calm macro conditions paired with persistent profit momentum, is exactly the kind of backdrop that sustains a bull market.

Where the Road Gets Bumpy

No forecast would be complete without discussing what might possibly go wrong. The immediate potential problem would be described as an “AI air pocket” by certain analysts. Should corporate investing in AI accelerate ahead of near-term monetization, there could be a temporary squeeze on margins, which in turn would put pressure on tech stocks with relatively high multiples. Such a development would not alter the long-term narrative but would suggest a steeper path.

Another factor would be monetary policy. If inflation slows but remains above target or if wage pressures re-accelerate, this would cause a pause in, or even reverse, planned monetary policy easing. This would present a challenge for growth stocks. Volatility in relation to the 2026 elections must also be watched, particularly in relation to deficit, rules, and/or trade rhetoric. Another area of risk that adds to all these uncertainties are valuations.

Another area of risk that contributes to these uncertainties are valuations. With the S&P 500 already above long-term averages, a substantial part of next year’s return has to come from earnings rather than further multiple expansion. This adds a certain amount of pressure on stocks. This is not a bearish message, but it certainly paints a tougher picture.

Geopolitical matters, and, of course, regulation, are wild cards. There are always potential flashpoints in matters of China-US relations related to boosted semiconductors, export restrictions, and hardware used in computation for AI. Some other conflict areas, as well as certain trends of regulation, also remain as sources of uncertainties, which are likely to affect markets mostly in terms of fluctuations, but not in a declining manner.

Takeaway

Once all the dots are connected, the outlook for 2026 becomes much easier to see. Earnings momentum builds. AI turns from promise to profit. Macro trends soften. Policy support persists. Market dominance expands. Stocks look rich but not unreasonable. An S&P 500 target of 8,000 doesn't represent a stretch of imagination, but rather a reflection of a business cycle that has further to run.

There will be corrections. There will be sideways stretches. There will be points in which the story teeters. But for those investors prepared to take a position in a story of earnings-driven, AI-fueled growth, 2026 doesn't appear the end of a prolonged bull market but rather a new part of it.

data.title: Bank of Canada Watch
data.description:
BoC: Good Economic Rebound, But We Will Keep Our Options Open

The Bank of Canada on Wednesday decided to hold the policy rate at 2.25% unchanged, as expected by markets. However, what is notable is the Bank continues to show dovish bias given the recent rebound in economic data.

Dec 2025 Decision:

Decision: Hold

Overnight Rate: 2.25%

Key Message: If the outlook changes, we are prepared to respond.

The general message presented in BoC’s rate decision statement, as well as Governor Tiff Macklem's press conference introductory statement, can be summarized as “yes, but!” as they adopt cautionary reading on all the “good” data observed in Q3.

Commenting on the GDP rebound in Q3, the Bank cautions that the reading “largely reflected volatility in trade” and started referring to final domestic demand as an alternative indicator of the strength of the Canadian economy. Final domestic demand comprises household and government consumption as well as private and government investment and strips out all the external trades. This shows the Bank has reservations on the recent rebound.

Final Domestic Demand shows that the Canadian economy was flat in Q3 (or down 0.02%), indicating local demand is rather lackluster. This stands in stark contrast to the 0.6% rise in total GDP, which was boosted mostly by lower imports.

(We have previously noted that Private Domestic Final Purchase (PDFP), a sum of only household consumption and business gross fixed capital formation, shows a similar trend to Final Domestic Demand.)

However, the Bank also predicts that “growth in final domestic demand to resume” in Q4, but will be offset by an “anticipated decline in net exports.”

How about the three consecutive positive job growth since September? “Looking ahead, however, we’re seeing muted hiring intentions across the economy,” Macklem said in his opening statement.

So, while the BoC thinks that “the current policy rate is at about the right level to keep inflation close to 2%,” their messaging is still that they want to keep their options open. They don’t want the framing going forward to be a choice between holding or raising rates, especially against the backdrop that “the upcoming review of the Canada-United States-Mexico Agreement (CUSMA) is creating uncertainty for many businesses.”

“Uncertainty remains elevated. If the outlook changes, we are prepared to respond.” This line on the post-meeting statement should be interpreted as a signal that the BoC is still open to a rate cut if the economy faces further headwinds.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Adobe: The New PayPal Or The Next Google?
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Adobe Inc. (ADBE) just delivered another strong quarter, posting double-digit revenue growth, rising ARR, and expanding AI adoption.

Yet the market is increasingly treating Adobe like a mature, ex-growth incumbent and assigning it a smaller and smaller multiple.

In my opinion, Adobe is starting to look like “the new PayPal.”

This is a high-quality, cash-rich franchise that investors have fallen out of love with because growth has normalized and there’s nothing to get excited about.

But this is a mistake.

With AI now embedded across the portfolio, generative credits ramping, and ARR still growing above 10%, Adobe’s current valuation looks compelling for patient investors.

Q4 Earnings

Adobe’s Q4 FY25 results were solid across the board.

Q4 revenues increased to $6.19 billion, up 10% YoY, while non-GAAP EPS came in at $5.50, up 14%.

Operating cash flow for the quarter was just over $10 billion, and Adobe carried out nearly $12 billion in share repurchases, reducing shares outstanding by over 6%.

Subscription revenues remain the growth engine, and like most other companies in the tech space, Adobe is embracing AI.

Total new AI-influenced ARR now exceeds 1/3 of our overall book of business, as we integrate AI deeply into our solutions and continue to launch new AI-first offerings.

Source: Earnings Call.

On this note, it’s important to point out that Generative credit consumption increased 3x QoQ across Firefly, Express, and Creative Cloud.

MAUs for creative freemium offerings (Firefly, Express, Premiere Mobile, and others) exceeded 70 million, up over 35% year over year.

And to top things off, customers with ARR over $10 million grew more than 25% to over 150, with record bookings.

For 2026, Adobe guided for revenues of $25.9–$26.1 billion and non-GAAP EPS of $23.30–23.50 and an operating margin of around 45%.

Overall, a steadily growing company with good profitability that is giving back to shareholders.

But clearly not enough for investors.

Adobe Is The New PayPal

Both Adobe and PayPal (PYPL) share several characteristics:

  • They are entrenched platforms in their core categories (creative/marketing software vs. online payments).

  • They generate significant, recurring cash flows with attractive margins.

  • They are still growing at high single-digit to low double-digit rates at scale.

Yet, both have seen their multiples compress as investors focus on competition and “disruption” rather than cash and durability.

Indeed, we have seen their stocks move in similar directions over the past year.

For PayPal, the derating came as total payment volume growth slowed versus history and margins began to peak, while competition from Apple Pay, Stripe, BNPL, and bank apps dominated the narrative.

For Adobe, a similar pattern is emerging.

ARR growth around 11% in FY25 and guidance of 10.2% for FY26 is still very healthy, but no longer in the 15–20%+ range that commands a premium “compounder” multiple. On top of that, Adobe now faces very intense competition. Canva has done very well, Figma (FIG) is a strong competitor, and most notably, Microsoft (MSFT) Copilot and LLMs threaten to do what Adobe does at a fraction of the price.

This is why Adobe is starting to look like the new PayPal.

A franchise still performing well operationally but emotionally downgraded by the market from “structural winner” to “possibly disrupted incumbent.”

But will Adobe continue to underperform, or could we see it undergo an Alphabet (GOOGL) moment?

Adobe’s Alphabet Moment?

For the past year, Adobe has been treated by the market the way Alphabet was, which was once seen as a major loser from AI.

But Google proved it could ship, integrate, and monetize AI at scale across its entire ecosystem, triggering a narrative reset and a swift re-rating.

Adobe now sits at a similar potential turning point.

The company has quietly built one of the most comprehensive, vertically integrated AI stacks in the industry.

Firefly models, third-party model orchestration, generative credits, agentic interfaces, enterprise content automation, and full closed-loop marketing workflows.

Most importantly, though, this AI strategy is showing up in the numbers, not just in demos.

AI-influenced ARR is now more than one-third of the entire book, generative credit consumption is growing exponentially, and Q4 saw record enterprise deals tied directly to AI workflows.

The market still values Adobe as if it is fighting for survival against Canva, Figma, and Copilot.

But if investors begin to view Adobe the way they now view Alphabet, an AI platform, not a legacy incumbent, the multiple can expand meaningfully.

Valuation

A shift from a 15x P/E “ex-growth software” narrative toward a 20–22x “AI-enhanced compounder” framework is entirely plausible if ARR growth holds above 10% and AI monetization continues to scale.

With a PEG below 1 and a forward PEG of 1.10, Adobe looks like a no-brainer right now.

On a free cash flow basis, the yield is attractive for a company of this quality.

With modest capex needs compared with infrastructure-heavy AI names, a high proportion of operating cash flow can be used to give back directly to investors.

Adobe can invest, buy back shares, and still maintain balance sheet flexibility.

In my view, Adobe is a category leader, with diversified revenue streams and a visible AI monetization path.

That combination is why I see the valuation as compelling rather than stretched.

Risks

The PayPal analogy is also a warning. There are real risks that could justify the market’s caution.

If LLMs plus low-cost tools can deliver “good enough” creative outputs for a wide range of use cases, some of Adobe’s customer base could trade down to cheaper alternatives.

Adobe’s answer is to own the professional workflow and the content supply chain, not just the one-off image or document.

That is the right strategy, but execution will determine whether the moat widens or erodes.

If ARR growth falls below the 10% threshold, investors will question whether AI is truly additive or just defensive, and this is an issue when faced with intense competition.

Final Thoughts

Adobe is the new PayPal in the sense that sentiment has overshot to the downside while the underlying franchise remains strong.

If management simply executes on its own plan—10% ARR growth, 45% margins, disciplined AI roll-out, and continued buybacks—today's valuation should look cheap in hindsight.

For investors willing to look beyond the noise around Canva, Figma, and LLMs, Adobe at a mid-teens multiple of forward earnings is a must-own.

data.title: Oracle: Calling Tops And Bottoms Is Much Easier Than You Think
data.description:
My opinion on Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) as a company has not changed much over the past five years since I first outlined my bullish investment thesis back in late 2020. Without getting into too much detail, the company was and still is positioned to be among the very few leaders in cloud infrastructure, database and ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) applications.

The stock, however, is an entirely different story. While ORCL was significantly undervalued when I covered it in October 2020, it has become fairly priced around mid-2023 and significantly overvalued earlier this year.

This different dynamic between each company and its stock is the reason why I wrote a thought piece called - "Oracle Blockbuster Earnings: Separating The Business From The Stock Price". It highlights the necessity for thinking about the two as objects which could often result in loving the company or hating the stock and vice versa.

As it happens, since I wrote the aforementioned article, ORCL stock fell by roughly 40% in a matter of just three months. Not only that, but without me trying to intentionally time the market, the article marked the short-term, and most likely to be the long-term, top of ORCL stock at around $328 at the time of publishing.

To me, it was quite obvious that ORCL was running way ahead of its actual business fundamentals and the 359% jump in remaining performance obligations (RPO) in Q1 2026 results was not a reason enough for the stock to skyrocket to a price-to-earnings ratio of nearly 80. That is why I ended my article with the following concluding thoughts.

Although Q1 2026 was yet another strong period for the company, the share price response is not justified. From here on, future shareholder returns are subject to very high risk and uncertainty (...)

Source: Seeking Alpha

In hindsight, calling the bottom of ORCL stock back in 2020 and its top a few months ago was an unintentional consequence of my applying my judgment based on the facts and refusing to go along with the predominant narratives and sentiment of the market.

It was quite obvious that the company was taking the right steps for its long-term success in the industry back in 2020. To the same extent, it was true that this success has finally come in recent years, but the market has become too optimistic in extrapolating short-term growth figures way beyond the reasonable timeline.

Contrary to all that, trend-setting sell-side analysts have done the exact opposite. They were very skeptical of the stock in late 2020 when the consensus rating fell to record lows. Just for the ratings to hit record highs earlier this year.

And as it oftentimes happens in the equity market, excessive optimism losses steam by some "surprising" event that was impossible to predict. Thus, as ORCL reported yet another very good quarter yesterday, the market commentators are now up in arms with and RPO that grew by only 15% sequentially.

As a result the stock is now down by almost 15% on the day following the earnings release and this is blamed on the results that failed to meet the excessively optimistic expectations.

All that perfectly illustrates how investors could take advantage of emotional biases and sentiment in the equity market. It is important, however, to refrain from following exciting and doomsday narratives and instead to look at each business in isolation to what the market thinks the share price should be.

Having said all that, investors in ORCL should now avoid falling victim to those emotional biases once again.

Don't Fall Victim To Biases Twice

Apparently investors' emotional biases are now influencing decisions regarding ORCL share price. Making investment decisions during such periods is quite tricky as the stock could deviate significantly from its fair value in either direction.

The second quarter report that just came out yesterday is by no means a poor one. Cloud Infrastructure revenue grew by a staggering 68%, compared to 54% in the prior 3-month period. Growth in the software-as-a-service segment remained resilient at 11% - the same rate as in Q1 2026.

Given these results and the fact that ORCL now trades 41% lower in just three months, one could easily be led to the conclusion that the stock is undervalued. And while short-term rebounds are possible, such a conclusion could lead to very disappointing results.

The first reason is that the flurry of speculative trading of ORCL in recent months which created significant volatility in the share price. Although one might think that this makes it easier to benefit from short-term movements, the reality is that the stock could remain significantly over- or undervalued for prolonged periods of time just to move violently in the other direction at some point in the future.

Secondly, even after the recent drop ORCL still trades at a price-earnings multiple of around 40, while its GAAP margins are much lower to the ones reported pre-Cerner acquisition. Amortization of acquired intangibles has been the key reason for the drop in margins, but these expenses have declined in significance in recent quarters and they now represent less than 10% of the reported operating income figure.

Lastly, ORCL is likely to remain subject to volatility as the company has now gone all-in on cloud infrastructure investments. While it is true that this strategy would secure the company's competitive positioning in the cloud and AI space for years into the future, but actual return on investment which ultimately matters for shareholder returns is far from certain.

Moreover, the relative share of cloud investments has now exceeded even the wildest estimates and with that ORCL shareholders are now entering uncharted territory. Quarterly capital expenditures now stand at almost 80% of revenue and to make matters even less predictable these investments are most likely heavily influenced by political decisions as well.

Conclusion

Based on everything said so far, anyone proclaiming that ORCL is now either significantly over- or undervalued over the long-run is most likely influenced by emotional biases. Currently, the stock is subject to extreme volatility and uncertainty which makes investment decisions exceptionally hard and prone to costly mistakes. Therefore, although there are certainly periods when we could say with a degree of certainty that ORCL is either over- or undervalued (as it was three months ago or in late 2020), this time period is not now.

data.title: Realty Income: Attractive Valuation And Continuing To Boost 'Income' With Options
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

Realty Income (O) is a popular dividend investment for income investors thanks to its consistently growing monthly payout. This name generally needs little introduction for most income investors. I hold a position in this name that I consider a core long-term holding. However, I've also been utilizing selling options in a way to 'boost' my income further and increase my overall cash flow.

It was about 3 months ago that we shared the last trade that we did, and during that time, shares of O have declined a bit. That's resulted in negative total returns for the last quarter, roughly.

However, that is completely fine for the short strangle options strategy that we've been employing.

The Latest Trades

While the passive income method of simply sitting back and collecting the monthly dividend is appealing, doing just a bit of work every quarter, we've been able to generate additional cash flow. Here is the latest moves we made:

  • First: Buy to Close Realty Income (O) December 19, 2025 $62.50 Calls - cost $0.10
  • Second: Sell to Open Realty Income (O) March 20, 2026 $60 Calls - collected $0.79
  • Third: Buy to Close Realty Income (O) December 19, 2025 $52.50 Puts - cost $0.10
  • Fourth: Sell to Open Realty Income (O) March 20, 2025 $55 Puts - collected $1.11

The total net premium received for rolling this short strangle is $1.70.

Shares of O were not moving too much on the day of the trade, and a lack of too much movement since entering our short strangle position means that most of the profit can be booked ahead of next week's expiration.

This is now the fifth time we'll be rolling our short strangle position with Realty Income. I've been rolling these throughout the entire year of 2025 since taking an assigned position of O shares in late 2024 at a strike of $57.50. That created the 'trading' batch of shares on top of what I hold as a core position as well.

These rolls have been a quarter out each time, making it a bit of a longer duration trade on these, but that also benefits by bringing in a larger option premium upfront. As long as we remain long shares, we are also collecting the monthly dividend.

With this latest move, we are once again going out another quarter, to the March 20, 2026 expiration date, 101 days away. However, shares have been a bit softer recently, so I'm moving the covered call strike down to $60 from the $62.50 level we had done in the previous trade.

According to Fidelity, we are looking at a probability of ~67% that shares will finish below that level come expiration time.

For our short put position, I'm still comfortable with potentially adding at $55, and it still provides a decent premium at that level. That narrows the range of our short strangle strikes, but according to Fidelity, the probability that shares should finish above $55 comes in at just over 75%.

Risks to this strategy would include if shares of O drop precipitously, we would be on the hook to purchase shares at $55—even if shares fall much further than that price.

On the other hand, if shares of O rally sharply higher, above $60, we'd also be potentially seeing the shares called away at $60. This is why I own a core position plus what I call the 'trading' batch of shares that I'm okay with seeing them get called away.

Alternatively, either to the downside or upside, there is also the ability to roll options. So that can be another way to avoid assignment potentially on either of the legs, by rolling up/down and/or out. To close out an in-the-money option generally means doing so at a loss based on intrinsic value.

Interestingly, though, thanks to the power of time decay (theta decay), that isn't always the case, as you can collect a high premium with enough extrinsic value initially that a position can be in-the-money but closed or rolled at a profit still on some occasions.

Recap of Previous Trades

With this latest move, we have now locked in a full year of trading options with O since taking assignment from writing puts in late 2024. Here is a recap of all previous and now the latest two trades, including the original puts assigned:

Ticker Expiration Date Upper Strike Current Price Move Until Strike Type Sold Date Initiated Premium Collected Date Closed Closing Cost Gain/Loss
O 03/20/2026 $60.00 $57.22 4.86% Calls 12/09/2025 $0.79 TBD TBD TBD
O 03/20/2026 $55.00 $57.22 -3.88% Puts 12/09/2025 $1.11 TBD TBD TBD
O 12/19/2025 $62.50 Rolled - Calls 09/19/2025 $0.62 12/09/2025 $0.10 $0.52
O 12/19/2025 $55.00 Rolled - Puts 09/19/2025 $0.82 12/09/2025 $0.10 $0.72
O 09/19/2025 $62.50 Rolled - Calls 06/10/2025 $0.72 09/19/2025 $0.04 $0.68
O 09/19/2025 $52.50 Rolled - Puts 06/10/2025 $0.43 09/19/2025 $0.05 $0.38
O 06/20/2025 $60.00 Rolled - Calls 03/21/2025 $0.58 06/10/2025 $0.03 $0.55
O 06/20/2025 $52.50 Rolled - Puts 03/21/2025 $0.98 06/10/2025 $0.07 $0.91
O 03/21/2025 $60.00 Rolled - Calls 12/18/2024 $0.45 03/21/2025 $0.03 $0.43
O 03/21/2025 $52.50 Rolled - Puts 12/18/2024 $1.20 03/21/2025 $0.03 $1.17
O 01/17/2025 $45.00 Expired - Puts 11/29/2023 $1.80 Expired - $1.80
O 12/20/2025 $57.50 Assigned - Puts 09/11/2024 $0.90 Assigned $0.90

With that original assignment, we saw the shares put to us at $57.50, but the $0.90 collected meant a breakeven of $56.60 We then intiailly wrote some more puts as the price of O saw significant downward pressure, allowing us to write puts at $45—which those expired worthless.

Disregarding that follow-up short put position, we've collected a total of $6.26 in option premiums from the initial assigned put trade and the short strangle trades following that, which are closed. When including this latest round, the premium comes up to $8.1,6, but it isn't 'locked' in yet until these latest legs expire or we roll again.

From writing those initial puts and then turning to covered calls, we've collected $3.08 or $3.87 with including the latest covered call trade. With that, the breakeven of these shares moves from the $57.50 strike to $53.63. During this time, the long shares have also collected another $3.217 from the monthly dividend.

Quick Updates on O

The dividend is one of the more appealing aspects of Realty Income, as it has a long history of growing its monthly dividend. It isn't always rapid growth, with the last 5-year CAGR coming in at 3.5%; however, it is consistent, as they have a long track record of raising every quarter. In fact, that latest was just announced on the 9th.

One of the things that has been pressuring O has been higher interest rates, on both the short and long ends. With the Fed cutting its benchmark rate, we are moving into an easing period that can bode well for REITs such as O. On the other hand, they can't control the long end, but that has still been somewhat stable as well.

Higher rates are a headwind for REIT investments because they tend to be viewed as income-focused investments. With O, that is very much the case as they've grown so large that rapid FFO and AFFO growth are likely long behind it. They need to make massive acquisitions just to move the needle a bit.

So instead, they focus more on rent bumps to keep earnings on a basis that is trending higher—which they have been able to deliver steady growth, even if it isn't remarkable.

Therefore, as O is quite mature at this point, I'd firmly categorize it as an income-focused REIT. Keeping AFFO climbing steadily means room to keep growing the dividend as well.

So, in terms of total returns, that becomes more critical to achieve. The dividend is likely to be the highest-returning portion for a shareholder when it comes to the long term, rather than seeing significant share price appreciation—that isn't to say that it won't happen, but it is less likely to see massive price increases.

With that, it makes sense that O's dividend yield of ~5.7% is higher than its average for the last decade, which comes in at 4.75%.

It is also trading below its longer-term average P/NAV per share, as it trades right near the ~$58 consensus NAV median estimate from analysts.

Finally, on the valuation front and as we would expect after seeing the average yield and price/NAV consensus, the P/AFFO is showing a historically attractive valuation level as well.

Again, this is all to be expected in a higher-rate environment. That's why we've seen its valuation come down from the average levels that it was able to achieve years ago when rates were at or near zero. With that, stabilizing long-term rates has been a positive for REITs, and lower short-term rates can also help to support these investments.

Conclusion

Given the attractive valuation, it's why I continue to find names like Realty Income attractive for a long-term core position. I also don't mind playing around with writing options when the opportunity presents itself to help 'boost' my quarterly cash flow in addition to the monthly dividend received. The strategy has its own unique risks and rewards and isn't going to be appropriate for all investors.

data.title: The Fed Made Its Third Cut Of The Year; Expectations Are Brightly Flashing
data.description:
For the last time in 2025, the Fed cut interest rates by a quarter of a percentage point. The target range for the federal funds rate now sits at 3.50-3.75%. Fed rate cuts can impact the markets and your investments in many ways. This applies to bonds, stocks and the strategy that you are using for your portfolio. Heading into the meeting, members of the Federal Open Market Committee pointed to inflation and signs of a weakening labor market as factors that could, and did, influence their decision.

When inflation rises, policymakers typically respond by raising the federal funds rate, which puts downward pressure on inflation by reducing consumer demand. When unemployment rises, policymakers try to boost the economy by lowering the inflation rate. Inflation remains above the Fed’s 2% target and has been ticking up this year. However, the unemployment rate has also risen recently, leaving officials in a quandary. In its FOMC minutes, the Fed acknowledged that inflation remains “somewhat elevated,” but also warned that unemployment could rise further. They are caught between a rock and a hard place.

According to the Fed’s statement, most officials think at least one more rate cut will be appropriate in 2026. Consumers could see interest rates on certain products inch down in anticipation of a cut. This, however, is pure speculation, as new members of the Fed will be appointed by the President in the upcoming year. After the cut was announced, the DJIA closed +497, while Treasury yields dipped. In the weeks leading up to this announcement, market prognosticators made a wide variety of market calls, and yesterday we got the actual answer.

Even with the release of the Fed’s latest projections, it’s too soon to say for certain whether the central bank will continue lowering rates next year. According to CME FedWatch, an additional 50 basis points in cuts by the end of 2026 are likely, but that could change depending on the state of the economy and by who is appointed to the Fed.

Changes to the federal funds rate can affect the rates offered on consumer loans like mortgages, auto loans, personal loans and credit cards. Exactly how interest rates are impacted, though, depends on the type of loan, the length of the loan and the purpose for which the loan is being used. If you have a loan with a fixed interest rate, your monthly payment won’t be affected by this latest Fed move, since your rate is already set. If you have a variable-rate loan or are shopping for a new loan, the Fed cut could help you get a slightly lower monthly payment. You might also see some lowering of interest rates on credit cards, but the American banks in general have not lowered rates regardless of the Fed’s prognostications. If you have money in a CD, you might soon earn less interest in those funds as banks adjust their savings rates even as they are keeping their borrowing rates quite high, in my opinion.

Stocks jumped and bonds dipped immediately following the Fed’s announcement, as I commented earlier, but we could see continued volatility as investors digest the commentary and forward guidance coming out of the meeting. One thing is for sure: if you have your money in a high-yield savings account, or a CD is coming due, you should shop the market to get the best rate possible. Different banks have widely divergent rates now. Rates vary from bank to bank, and just because your current bank had the best rate when you initially opened an account doesn’t mean that it still does.

This was the Fed’s last meeting of 2025, and it won’t meet again until January. In the intervening time, markets will closely watch the latest data and public remarks from policymakers, our politicians, our President and members of the Fed to understand whether more rate cuts are likely in 2026. Given President Trump’s recent pronouncements, I would expect more Fed cuts in the coming year. That is my opinion.

One of the next major reports to come out will be the November jobs report on December 16, followed by November’s consumer price index data release on December 18. Higher inflation typically makes it less likely that the Fed will cut rates, while a weaker labor market makes cuts more likely. Once these reports and other data are made public, we’ll be able to better sense what the economy and the markets may do in the forthcoming year.

While I am still in favor of investing for income, I also recognize that appreciation should be a part of your thinking when considering your investments. I also think the effects of AI will begin to wear off soon, as many strategists have already included the upcoming changes in the economy given the broader knowledge of what AI will do to the economy. My monthly income strategy will also help, as it provides new cash each month that can be invested based upon what is happening in different sectors of the markets. I remain cautious, but I am also more positive about our markets than I was several months ago.

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
